criteria: Implement simd_memset using _mm_set1_epi8 to create fill pattern and _mm_store_si128 for aligned 16-byte stores
Implement simd_memcpy using _mm_loadu_si128/_mm_store_si128 pairs with destination alignment
Handle buffer edges with scalar prologue (align to 16-byte boundary) and scalar epilogue (remaining 0-15 bytes)
Fall back to pure scalar implementation for buffers smaller than 16 bytes
Implement simd_memset_stream variant using _mm_stream_si128 with _mm_sfence fence
Benchmark scalar, simd_memset, simd_memcpy, and libc memset/memcpy across sizes (64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB)
Disable compiler auto-vectorization for scalar baseline using __attribute__((optimize("no-tree-vectorize")))
Document benchmark methodology: CPU frequency pinning, warmup runs, median of 10+ iterations
Explain why libc may outperform hand-written SSE2 (AVX/AVX-512 usage, non-temporal stores, CPU-specific tuning)
Identify buffer size threshold where non-temporal stores provide measurable benefit vs standard stores
Implement simd_strlen using _mm_cmpeq_epi8 to compare 16 bytes against zero in parallel, with _mm_movemask_epi8 to extract comparison results and __builtin_ctz to find the first null position
Implement simd_memchr using _mm_set1_epi8 to broadcast the target byte, _mm_cmpeq_epi8 for parallel comparison, with proper size limit handling via scalar prologue/epilogue

Use the aligned-read-with-masking technique for page-boundary safety: when the input pointer is not 16-byte aligned, either use scalar prologue OR read from the aligned-down address and shift/mask the comparison result
Verify correctness with test cases covering lengths 0, 1, 15, 16, 17, 63, 64, 65, 255, 256, 4095, 4096, 4097 and alignment offsets 0-15
Test page-boundary cases: strings ending at the last byte of a mapped page with the next page unmapped should not crash
Benchmark against libc strlen/memchr across string lengths and report median latency with proper warmup and CPU frequency pinning
Understand why libc wins: glibc uses SSE4.2 pcmpistri or AVX2 with decades of optimization; the learning goal is understanding the patterns, not beating libc
Implement SIMD dot product using _mm_mul_ps for element-wise multiply followed by shuffle+add horizontal reduction (_mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ps), NOT _mm_hadd_ps
Implement 4x4 float matrix multiplication using SSE intrinsics, processing row-column dot products with proper accumulator management
Implement both row-major and column-major matrix layouts with benchmarks demonstrating the cache impact of access patterns (expect 2-3× difference for larger matrices)
Implement AVX 256-bit variants of dot product using _mm256_mul_ps, _mm256_add_ps, and proper 256-to-128 reduction via _mm256_extractf128_ps
Insert _mm256_zeroupper() before any transition from AVX to SSE code paths, or use __attribute__((target("avx"))) for AVX-only functions
Implement runtime CPU feature detection using __get_cpuid or __builtin_cpu_supports to select optimal code path at runtime
Benchmark scalar vs SSE vs AVX across array sizes (4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements for dot product) with proper methodology (warmup, median of 10+ runs)
Achieve at least 2× speedup for SSE and 4× speedup for AVX over scalar baseline (no-tree-vectorize) for 1024+ element dot products
Verify numerical correctness using relative error comparison between SIMD and scalar results (epsilon < 1e-5)
Document FMA availability and its impact on dot product performance for CPUs that support it
Implement SIMD dot product using _mm_mul_ps for element-wise multiply, then reduce using shuffle+add pattern (_mm_shuffle_ps + _mm_add_ps), NOT _mm_hadd_ps
Implement 4x4 float matrix multiplication using SSE, processing one row-column dot product per iteration
Support both row-major and column-major matrix layouts; benchmark both to demonstrate access pattern impact
Implement AVX (256-bit) variants of both operations using _mm256_* intrinsics
Insert _mm256_zeroupper() or use __attribute__((target("avx"))) to avoid AVX-SSE transition penalties when mixing code paths
Implement runtime CPU feature detection using CPUID (or compiler builtins) to select SSE or AVX code path
Benchmark scalar vs SSE vs AVX across array sizes (4, 16, 64, 256, 1024 elements for dot product; various matrix sizes)
Show at least 2x speedup for SSE and 3x for AVX over scalar for large inputs (1024+ elements)
